We divide the comments into major and minor anchors as follows. The comments Author = Mr. Speaker, Author = ill. le Pr(sident, Author = Some Hon. Members, and Author = Des Voix are called minor anchors. All other comments are called major anchors with the exception of the Paragraph comment which is not treated as an anchor at all. He was a young man of vigour, ability, and resolution, and immediately applied himself to remove some of the evils which had arisen in the last unhappy reign. He revoked all the grants of land that had been hastily made, on either side, during the late struggles; he obliged numbers of disorderly soldiers to depart from England; he reclaimed all the castles belonging to the Crown; and he forced the wicked nobles to pull down their own castles, to the number of eleven hundred, in which such dismal cruelties had been inflicted on the people. The King's brother, Geoffrey, rose against him in France, while he was so well employed, and rendered it necessary for him to repair to that country; where, after he had subdued and made a friendly arrangement with his brother (who did not live long), his ambition to increase his possessions involved him in a war with the French King, Louis, with whom he had been on such friendly terms just before, that to the French King's infant daughter, then a baby in the cradle, he had promised one of his little sons in marriage, who was a child of five years old. However, the war came to nothing at last, and the Pope made the two Kings friends again.

The tree outside my window is committing plagiarism for the twentieth year in a row, adding green leaves to green leaves. The flowers of the cherry tree are no different from the first cherry tree; the same smell as yesterday permeates the air. And—though old people say this is tedious—my sister is kissing someone under the same tree where I used to kiss, endlessly plagiarizing the first kiss. I could still tell you about the grasses, all the grasses that sprouted from seeds faithfully and persistently, the same, the exact same grass as months ago. The world is not afraid to plagiarize when making new life, and always equally astonishing and monotonous in its stubbornness is death. Why then condemn poems of love, why blame them for their lack of shame and their primitive, chaotic groans of pleasure, faithfully replicated for centuries, indifferent to who reads them?

Finally, we consider the issue of negation. If a tree does not satisfy some relationship then it satisfies the negation of that relationship, and vice versa. For quasi-trees the situation is more subtle. Viewing the quasi-tree as a set of trees, if every tree in that set fails to satisfy some relationship, then they all satisfy the negation of that relationship. Hence the quasi-tree must satisfy the negated relationship as well. On the other hand, viewing the quasi-tree as a set of relationships, if a particular relationship is not included in the quasi-tree it does not imply that none of the trees it describes satisfies that relationship, only that some of those trees do not. Thus it may be the case that a quasi-tree neither satisfies a relationship nor satisfies its negation.

This paper presents a new approach for resolving lexical ambiguities in one language using statistical data on lexical relations in another language. This approach exploits the differences between mappings of words to senses in different languages. We concentrate on the problem of target word selection in machine translation, for which the approach is directly applicable, and employ a statistical model for the selection mechanism. The model was evaluated using two sets of Hebrew and German examples and was found to be very useful for disambiguation.

In the opening section of this presentation, I will examine one of the fundamental mechanisms that enable metaphorical extension: the semantic process of "property abstraction," which permits the attribution of characteristics belonging to one domain to entities in another, and which accounts for a significant portion of figurative language in everyday discourse. The phenomenon I wish to discuss can be demonstrated through examples such as "The music is really sharp today." This is commonly analyzed as a straightforward case of sensory transfer—where auditory properties are described using vocabulary originally associated with visual or tactile experience—whereby we apply an expression from one sensory sphere to characterize phenomena in an entirely different perceptual realm. However, I will argue that upon closer examination, the expression does not simply borrow vocabulary from another domain; rather, the underlying semantic structure reveals something more complex about how we conceptualize cross-modal experience. Consider the fact that adjectives like "sharp" behave differently when applied to sounds than they do when applied to visual stimuli: while we can speak of a sharp image or a sharp outline, the sharpness attributed to sound concerns temporal precision and clarity of definition, not visual acuity. Additional phonological and semantic patterns corroborate this analysis: the metaphorical mapping preserves certain systematic constraints while relaxing others, suggesting that what is at stake is not mere associative substitution but a structured projection of conceptual content. In other words, when we describe music as sharp, we are accessing a specialized sense of the term that has been abstracted from its original perceptual domain and adapted to capture specific qualities of auditory experience that our native vocabulary for sound does not otherwise adequately express.

Through iterative comparison between the system’s proposed bracketings and the correct bracketings supplied in a training corpus, the model gradually acquires a collection of simple structural transformations designed to minimize parsing errors. Upon scrutinizing the Hansard archives, we discovered that the annotations scattered within can function as pivotal reference points for the alignment procedure. We classify these annotations into primary and secondary markers as follows. The annotations tagged as Author = Honorable Speaker, Author = Esteemed President, Author = Various Honorable Members, and Author = General Voices are designated as secondary markers. Conversely, all other remarks are categorized as primary markers, with the exception of the Paragraph annotation, which does not qualify as an anchor point. The secondary markers outnumber the primary ones significantly, rendering an alignment reliant on them less resilient to omissions compared to an approach anchored by primary markers. Consequently, we executed the alignment of marker points in two stages: initially, aligning the primary markers, followed by the secondary markers.

Melancholy streets, in a penitential garb of soot, steeped the souls of the people who were condemned to look at them out of windows, in dire despondency. In every thoroughfare, up almost every alley, and down almost every turning, some doleful bell was throbbing, jerking, tolling, as if the Plague were in the city and the dead-carts were going round. Everything was bolted and barred that could by possibility furnish relief to an overworked people. No pictures, no unfamiliar animals, no rare plants or flowers, no natural or artificial wonders of the ancient world—all taboo with that enlightened strictness, that the ugly South Sea gods in the British Museum might have supposed themselves at home again. Nothing to see but streets, streets, streets. Nothing to breathe but streets, streets, streets. Nothing to change the brooding mind, or raise it up. Firstly, we will address the aspect of negation. If a tree does not meet a certain relationship, it means that it meets the opposite of that relationship, and the opposite is also true. In the case of quasi-trees, the situation is more complex. When we look at the quasi-tree as a group of trees, if each tree in that group fails to meet a specific relationship, then they all meet the opposite of that relationship. So, the quasi-tree must meet the opposite relationship as well. However, when we see the quasi-tree as a group of relationships, if a certain relationship is not included in the quasi-tree, it does not mean that none of the trees it describes meet that relationship, only that some of those trees do not. Thus, it may be possible that a quasi-tree neither meets a relationship nor meets its opposite.

For this purpose, a selection of 94 Verb+Particle/Preposition pairs was compiled to reflect a spectrum of usage frequencies, ranging from predominantly noun-based to predominantly verb-based. From each pair, 20 example sentences were randomly drawn from an online corpus known as MODERN, which aggregates multiple corpora including Brown, WSJ, AP88-92, HANSE, HAROW, WAVER, DOE, NSF, TREEBANK, and DISS, collectively comprising over 400 million words. These sentences were initially annotated manually to establish a reference standard, followed by automatic tagging using PARTS. Additionally, the pre-existing assumption of assigning only a verbal tag to all the pairs was also tested to determine whether this straightforward method would be suitable across all instances. The performance of the three tagging methods was then compared and analyzed.

In the first part of this talk, I'll focus on one of the general processes that underlie transfer: the semantic operation of "predicate transfer," which licenses the sortal reassignment of expressions denoting properties and relations, and which underlies a great deal of lexical polysemy. The process I have in mind is illustrated by an example like "I am parked out back." This is usually regarded as a classic instance of metonymy- i.e.,"person" for "car" -- where we use an expression that would conventionally denote one thing to refer to some other thing to which it is connected by a "relation of contiguity." But I'll show that on consideration there are compelling reasons for supposing that I here refers to the speaker, rather than his car. (For one thing the number of the pronoun doesn't vary according to the number of cars involved: if you had two cars parked out back you wouldn't say "We are parked out back," though of course this would be the appropriate thing to say to refer to a single car owned by two or more people). And other morphological and syntactic observations support the same conclusion: in examples like this what has been transferred is the meaning of the predicate, rather than its argument. That is, the predicate parked out back has a transferred reading here: it denotes the property that the speaker acquires in virtue of his relation to a car that has the property of being parked out back.
